title: Saint Anger Stories From Christian Mystics
dek: *The saints who shaped Western spirituality didn't transcend their anger through spiritual bypass. They wrestled with it in prayer cells and desert caves, discovering that holy rage could become a doorway to divine encounter when met with unflinching presence rather than pious denial.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Christian mystics viewed anger as sacred information pointing toward love wounded or justice denied, not sin to suppress. Their writings reveal contemplative practices that metabolize rage into prophetic clarity without bypassing the body's truth. These ancient techniques align remarkably with modern somatic psychology and offer practical paths through fury today.

The mystics kept notebooks. Between prayers and ecstatic visions, they recorded something the contemporary church often edited out: their fury. Not polite frustration or righteous indignation dressed in Sunday language, but the raw, body-shaking kind of anger that made Teresa of Avila throw her breviary across the room and tell God exactly what she thought of his timing. Here's what I've come to understand after years of reading their unredacted letters and private journals: these women and men didn't pray their anger away. They prayed it *through*. There's a difference, and it's the difference between spiritual constipation and actual transformation. ## The Desert Mothers and the Fury That Wouldn't Be Silenced Amma Syncletica, a fourth-century desert mother whose sayings survived in fragments, taught her students something that sounds almost blasphemous in certain church contexts. She said anger reveals what we love. When rage rises, she instructed, don't immediately confess it as sin. First, ask it what it's protecting. This wasn't permissiveness. The desert tradition knew that unexamined anger becomes poison. But examined anger, the kind you can feel in your jaw and solar plexus while you sit with it in prayer, that kind tells the truth about violated boundaries and trampled dignity. In my own practice, I've watched this play out dozens of times. A client comes in certain their anger is evidence of spiritual failure. We sit with the sensation, the heat behind the sternum, the tightness in the throat. Within ten minutes, the anger reveals its source: a mother who needed them silent, a church that demanded they shrink, a partner who mistook their kindness for an invitation to colonize their time. The desert mothers understood what somatic psychologist Pat Ogden would articulate fifteen centuries later: the body keeps the score. Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy demonstrates that emotions live in musculature and breath patterns, not just thoughts. The mystics knew this. They fasted not to punish the body but to hear it more clearly. ## John of the Cross and the Dark Night's Hidden Rage Most people know John of the Cross for his poetry about divine union. Fewer know about the nine months he spent imprisoned in a six-by-ten-foot cell in Toledo, beaten regularly by his Carmelite brothers who disagreed with his reforms. He was thirty-five years old. The poems he smuggled out weren't all about mystical sweetness. His "Dark Night of the Soul" contains passages where the absence of God feels like abandonment, and abandonment breeds a particular species of fury. John didn't pathologize this. He mapped it as territory every serious contemplative would cross. The anger at God's silence, the rage at spiritual dryness, the fury at a path that promised intimacy but delivered desolation. What's remarkable in his letters (across the articles we've published on this site, we've explored this theme repeatedly) is his instruction to stay present to the anger without acting it out or repressing it. He called it "passive purification," but there's nothing passive about sitting in a cell with your rage and choosing not to let it metastasize into bitterness. Thomas Moore, writing about care of the soul in contemporary contexts, echoes this when he suggests that depression and anger often arise when we've exiled parts of ourselves that need integration. The dark night isn't divine punishment. It's the soul's insistence that we stop pretending. ## Catherine of Siena's Letters: Fury as Prophetic Fuel Catherine wrote to popes. Not polite suggestions slipped under the door, but searing letters that called out corruption, cowardice, and the weaponization of doctrine to protect power. She signed them "I, Catherine" and told the most powerful men in Christendom they were failing God and the people. Her anger was specific. She named names. She detailed abuses. And she did it all while maintaining a contemplative life that included hours of silent prayer daily. This is the part modern anger-management frameworks often miss: discernment. Catherine knew the difference between reactive rage (which she called "disordered") and prophetic anger rooted in love for those being harmed. In our experience working with people who carry anger about systemic injustice, the Catherine model offers something crucial. Your fury at systems that grind people down isn't evidence you need more therapy. It might be evidence you're paying attention. The question isn't whether the anger is justified. The question is whether you're metabolizing it into action or letting it eat you from the inside. This distinction shows up in research too. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load demonstrates that suppressed anger creates more physiological damage than anger expressed appropriately. Catherine didn't suppress. She wrote, she spoke, she acted. Then she returned to silence to let the well refill. ## Julian of Norwich and the Anger at Suffering Julian's "Revelations of Divine Love" came during a near-death illness at age thirty. Between visions of Christ's passion, she wrestled with a question that still generates anger in anyone who's watched someone they love suffer pointlessly: why does God permit this? Her answer wasn't a theological treatise. It was a felt sense she struggled to put into Middle English: "All shall be well, and all shall be well, and all manner of thing shall be well." But she didn't arrive there by skipping over the anger. She spent years after her visions interrogating them, pushing back, demanding clarity. What emerges in her longer text (she wrote two versions, the second after decades of reflection) is a woman who refused cheap comfort. She wanted to understand how suffering and divine love coexist. Her anger at the question drove her into deeper contemplation, not away from it. Gabor Maté's work on trauma and illness suggests that unexpressed anger about early wounding often manifests as physical disease later. Julian's illness, viewed through this lens, becomes not just a mystical event but possibly her body's way of forcing her to confront questions she'd been taught not to ask. The visions gave her permission to be furious about suffering and to take that fury directly to God. ## A Five-Minute Practice: The Mystic's Anger Inventory Here's something you can do right now, adapted from the methods these mystics actually used: Sit somewhere you won't be interrupted. Place one hand on your heart, one on your belly. Close your eyes. Ask yourself: "What am I angry about today?" Not the surface irritations. The real thing underneath. When you locate it, don't analyze. Just feel where it lives in your body. Tight shoulders? Clenched jaw? Hot chest? Stay there for three full breaths. Then ask it, as Amma Syncletica taught: "What are you protecting? What do you love that's being threatened?" Let the answer come in images or sensations, not necessarily words. When you have it, thank the anger for showing up. It's information. Then ask: "What one small action could I take today that honors what this anger is protecting?" Write that action down. Do it before bed. This isn't catharsis. It's discernment. The mystics knew that anger left unattended becomes resentment, but anger interrogated becomes clarity. ## Francis of Assisi and the Rage Against Wealth The sanitized version of Francis shows a gentle man who talked to birds. The actual Francis staged a public confrontation with his father, stripped naked in the town square, and renounced his inheritance in front of the bishop and half of Assisi. That's not whimsy. That's fury at a system that valued cloth and coin over human dignity. His anger at wealth wasn't abstract. He'd seen what it did to his father, turning him into someone who could beat his son for giving bread to the poor. He'd watched the church accumulate land while people starved. His response was to build an entire religious movement on the principle of radical poverty. What's instructive here is that Francis channeled his anger into structure. He didn't just rage against wealth. He created an alternative economy where brothers shared everything and worked with their hands. His anger had enough heat to sustain decades of institution-building. That's different from the flash-fire rage that burns bright and burns out. Over the years I've seen this pattern: people whose anger leads somewhere tend to let it simmer rather than explode. They take it into prayer, into long walks, into journals. They let it clarify into vision. Then they build. Francis spent two years in a cave praying before he started his order. The anger was doing underground work. ## The Body Knows Before the Mind Admits There's a moment in Hildegard of Bingen's "Scivias" where she describes a vision of divine love as fire. But she also describes her own body's response to spiritual dryness: a feeling of being "pressed down" and "hardened." Modern readers might call this depression. Medieval mystics called it acedia, spiritual sloth. But Hildegard's letters reveal something else underneath: anger at God for withdrawing the felt sense of presence. She didn't spiritualize this away. She wrote about the physical sensations. The heaviness in her limbs. The thickness in her throat. The way her voice felt trapped. These are the same symptoms people describe in therapy when they talk about swallowed anger. Your body registers anger before your theology has time to label it inappropriate. The mystics trusted this. They paid attention to the knot in the gut, the heat in the face, the urge to throw something. Then they brought those sensations into prayer, not as things to be fixed but as information to be heard. I remember sitting in a chapel years ago, furious about something I can't even recall now. My hands were shaking. Instead of leaving or praying it away, I just sat there and felt it. Watched it move through my system like weather. After about twenty minutes, underneath the anger, I found grief. Underneath the grief, fear. Underneath the fear, a very old wound that had nothing to do with the triggering event. The anger was just the guard at the door. If you're someone whose nervous system runs hot, [NOW Foods - GABA with Vitamin B6 500 mg. - 200 Vegetarian Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00B0YA0K0?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help take the physiological edge off while you do the deeper work. But supplements are support, not substitutes for actually feeling what needs to be felt. ## The Mystics' Secret: Anger as Compass Here's what the mystics understood that most anger-management programs miss: anger points. It's a compass needle swinging toward true north. Teresa of Avila got angry when her nuns were treated as less-than by male clergy. That anger reformed Carmelite monasticism. Meister Eckhart got angry at empty ritual. That anger produced sermons that still challenge the church's power structures seven centuries later. The question isn't whether you should be angry. If you're alive and paying attention, you will be. The question is whether you're willing to follow the anger to its source and let it show you what needs to change. Not out there, necessarily. Sometimes the thing that needs to change is in here, in the architecture of your own assumptions. [The fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) we've developed draws heavily on these mystical practices, adapted for people who may not pray in traditional ways but still need methods for working with rage that don't involve either explosion or repression. The mystics offer a third way: accompaniment. You walk with your anger like a companion who knows something you don't yet. For those moments when anger has left your throat raw from speaking truth or crying, [Traditional Medicinals Throat Coat (16 bags)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00B77XTA8?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers gentle, herbal relief. Sometimes caring for the body is how we honor the emotional work we've done. ## Integration: When Anger Becomes Prayer There's a practice in some contemplative traditions of praying the Psalms, including the angry ones. Psalm 137 ends with a blessing on whoever smashes Babylonian babies against rocks. Psalm 109 is a sustained curse. These made it into scripture not because violence is holy, but because the writers understood that bringing your unedited rage to God is more honest than pretending it doesn't exist. The mystics prayed these psalms daily. They let the ancient words give shape to their own fury. Then they sat in silence and let God respond, or not respond, as God chose. This is different from using prayer as a pressure-release valve. It's using prayer as a container strong enough to hold what you actually feel. When I teach people to work with anger contemplatively, I often suggest they write their own angry psalm. Not for publication. Not even for keeping. Just to let the full truth of their fury have language. Then burn it, bury it, or keep it in a sealed envelope. The act of articulating rage in the presence of something larger than yourself (whether you call that God, the universe, or your own deepest wisdom) changes the rage's relationship to you. Some people find that anointing pulse points with [doTERRA Frankincense Essential Oil (15 mL)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003I6Q3IG?tag=spankyspinola-20) creates a sensory anchor for this kind of contemplative anger work. Frankincense has been used in spiritual practice for millennia, and scent is one of the fastest routes to shifting nervous system states. ## Questions People Actually Ask ### Can anger really be holy, or is that just justifying my bad behavior? Holy anger and destructive anger have different root systems. Holy anger arises from love for what's being harmed and moves toward protection or repair. It has a specific target and proportionate intensity. Destructive anger is diffuse, often rooted in old wounds, and seeks to punish rather than change. The mystics would say: check your fruit. If your anger is making you cruel, it's not holy. If it's making you braver in defense of the vulnerable, pay attention. ### How do I know if I should express my anger or keep it to myself? The mystics used discernment practices that involved time, counsel, and prayer. A good modern translation: wait twenty-four hours before acting on anger. Write out what you want to say. Share it with a trusted friend who'll tell you the truth. Ask yourself: is this in service of relationship and justice, or just venting? Catherine of Siena's letters were fierce, but she wrote them after years of contemplative formation. Reactivity and prophetic anger aren't the same thing. ### What if my spiritual community says I shouldn't be angry at all? Then you're in a community that hasn't read its own mystics very carefully. Every major figure in Christian contemplative tradition wrestled with anger, expressed anger, or used anger as fuel for reform. A spirituality that demands you amputate your anger is a spirituality that will eventually demand you amputate other true parts of yourself too. You might need to find teachers who can hold the complexity. [The about page](/about) explains more about the contemplative lineage we work from, which honors the full emotional spectrum as sacred data. ## The Invitation Anger Extends The mystics didn't resolve their anger. That's the uncomfortable truth their hagiographies often obscure. Teresa died frustrated that her reforms hadn't gone far enough. John of the Cross spent his last months marginalized by the order he'd helped transform. Catherine's letters to the pope didn't prevent the Western Schism. But their anger didn't make them bitter. It made them clarify what they loved and spend themselves in service of it. The fury became focus. The rage became resilience. They let their anger show them where their hearts were most alive, then oriented their entire lives toward those tender, fierce places. You're standing in that same choice point every time anger rises. You can pathologize it, weaponize it, or apprentice yourself to it as a teacher. The mystics chose apprenticeship. They let anger lead them into caves and cells and confrontations that cost them everything comfortable. And in those stripped-down places, they found a love fierce enough to hold both the anger and what the anger was protecting. That's the inheritance they left: not instructions for transcending your humanity, but a detailed map for inhabiting it so fully that even your fury becomes a way of touching the divine. If you're interested in exploring more practices that work with rather than against your emotional truth, [more articles in this journal](/articles) offer additional contemplative tools, and [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) provides companion resources for this kind of soul work. Your anger knows something. The mystics would tell you: stop long enough to let it speak. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on saint anger stories from christian mystics, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
